trajectory.description: Observation: Starting the experiment. Analysis: We need a baseline model to validate the pipeline and measure iteration time. DLinear is a simple, fast, and effective baseline for long-term time series forecasting. I will implement DLinear, train it for 5 epochs, and submit an evaluation. Decision: Write model.py, train.py, and predict.py, then run train.py and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: CUDA error 'no kernel image is available' indicates a mismatch between the installed PyTorch version and the GPU architecture. Analysis: To quickly get a baseline and measure iteration time, I will switch to CPU for training and prediction, as DLinear is very lightweight and trains fast even on CPU. I will also print the PyTorch and CUDA versions to diagnose the GPU issue for future iterations. Decision: Modify train.py and predict.py to use CPU, run the training, and submit the evaluation.

trajectory.description: Observation: The previous iteration took ~7 minutes for 5 epochs, which is slow for a simple linear model. The bottleneck was likely creating tensors on the fly in the Dataset `__getitem__`. Also, the validation loss was increasing, indicating overfitting or a learning rate that is too high. Analysis: Pre-converting the entire dataset to a PyTorch tensor in `__init__` will significantly speed up data loading. Switching to `individual=True` in DLinear (separate weights for each channel) usually improves performance on multivariate forecasting. Adding a learning rate scheduler and saving the best model based on validation loss will help achieve a better score. Decision: Update `train.py` to optimize the Dataset, add a scheduler, and save the best model. Update `predict.py` to use `individual=True`. Run for 15 epochs.

trajectory.command: python -c "import torch; from chronos import ChronosBoltPipeline; pipeline = ChronosBoltPipeline.from_pretrained('amazon/chronos-bolt-small', device_map='cpu'); context = [torch.randn(96) for _ in range(2)]; forecast = pipeline.predict(context, prediction_length=720); print('Chronos forecast shape:', forecast.shape)" ; python train.py

trajectory.description: Observation: The previous iteration took 29 minutes because the DLinear model with individual=True has a Python for-loop over channels and creates tensors on the fly, which is very slow on CPU. We only have ~22 minutes left in the time budget. Analysis: We need a much faster model. A simple Linear model with Reversible Instance Normalization (RevIN) and weight sharing across channels is mathematically similar to DLinear but blazingly fast (seconds per epoch). I will also run a quick inline test to check the output shape of the pre-downloaded Chronos-Bolt-Small model, in case we want to use it as a zero-shot predictor in the next iteration. Decision: Replace DLinear with a fast Linear+RevIN model, train for 10 epochs, and submit eval. Also run the Chronos test inline.

trajectory.description: Observation: The Linear+RevIN model achieved a new best MSE of 0.4797 and trained very quickly (the 433s included a slow inline Chronos test). Analysis: We have ~15 minutes left. We can improve the Linear model by adding a small MLP branch with a residual connection. This allows the model to learn non-linear patterns while retaining the strong linear baseline. We will also add weight decay and a learning rate scheduler, and train for 30 epochs. Decision: Update model to MLPModel (Linear + MLP residual), train for 30 epochs, and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: The previous run failed due to a TypeError with ReduceLROnPlateau's 'verbose' argument, which is deprecated in newer PyTorch versions. Analysis: I need to remove the 'verbose=True' argument from the scheduler initialization. I will also reduce the epochs to 25 to ensure we stay well within the remaining 15-minute time budget. Decision: Fix train.py, run for 25 epochs, and submit eval.
